title: Andys Ancestry
weakest_section: 18:00-19:00 range with fewer distinct jokes during Jim/Darryl conversation

jokes.text: Who am I? I'm Jim. We've been working together for 12 years. Weird joke, Dwight. You're not Jim. Jim's not Asian. You seriously never noticed? Hey, hats off to you for not seeing race.
jokes.setup: Dwight questions the identity of someone claiming to be Jim
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Jim hired an Asian actor to impersonate him, with the perfect deadpan 'hats off to you for not seeing race'
jokes.explanation: Masterful prank setup with perfect character integration - this is exactly the kind of elaborate, long-term prank Jim would execute. The 'not seeing race' line is brilliant misdirection that reframes Dwight's confusion as accidental progressivism.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Wait, who are you? You're not Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Asian Jim
dialogue.line: I am Jim Halpert.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No, you're not. Jim is...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, meet Asian Jim. I hired him to prove a point.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't understand. Why would you—

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hats off to you for not seeing race.

jokes.text: Asian Jim knows Jim's sales details and voicemail password
jokes.setup: Dwight tests the impostor Jim with work knowledge
jokes.punchline: Asian Jim knows intimate work details, escalating the impossibility of the situation
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the prank - the thoroughness of Jim's preparation makes it funnier and more unbelievable for Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What is the password to the sales database?

dialogue.speaker: Asian Jim
dialogue.line: It's your mother's maiden name followed by your first car.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's... correct. But anyone could know that.

dialogue.speaker: Asian Jim
dialogue.line: Your desk drawer has a hidden compartment behind the false bottom. You keep a gun there. And a half-eaten cheesecake from 1997.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: How did you—

dialogue.speaker: Asian Jim
dialogue.line: You talk in your sleep. And I've been in your dreams.

jokes.text: Pam treats Asian Jim as her husband, making dinner reservations
jokes.setup: Dwight is already confused by Asian Jim
jokes.punchline: Pam casually treats Asian Jim as her husband, making the situation even more impossible
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that involves Pam in the prank. The casual domesticity makes it even more surreal for Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Hey babe, how was your day?

dialogue.speaker: Asian Jim
dialogue.line: Good, honey.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What is happening right now?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I'm just asking my husband about his day.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: But... Jim is right there.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I know. This is Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No, that's not—

dialogue.speaker: Asian Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, why are you being so weird about this?

jokes.text: Dwight's confused stammering reaction when he sees the real Jim
jokes.setup: Dwight has been dealing with Asian Jim all morning
jokes.punchline: Dwight's brain breaks when he sees both Jims
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to the prank - Dwight's complete mental breakdown is the perfect victim reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What? How is this possible? There's two of you!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Surprise!

dialogue.speaker: Asian Jim
dialogue.line: We've been pranking you all morning.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This is... this is impossible. My mind... I can't... there's TWO Jims.

jokes.text: This morning, I brushed my teeth in the shower. Saved myself 90 seconds. Which I just used to explain this to you. Damn it.
jokes.setup: Darryl is trying to be more efficient with life hacking
jokes.punchline: He immediately wastes the time he saved by explaining how he saved it
jokes.explanation: Solid structure with good self-awareness. The immediate undermining of his efficiency gains is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I've been life hacking. You know, little tricks to save time throughout the day.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: For instance, I used to spend 45 minutes every morning deciding what to wear. Now I just wear the same thing every day.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Saves me almost an hour a day.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Which I then spend explaining to people how I saved the time.

jokes.text: Whoa. That person has really gotten him... or herself... into quite a predicament.
jokes.setup: Darryl is practicing corporate speak
jokes.punchline: His overly formal, gender-neutral description of someone in trouble
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Darryl's awkward attempts at corporate professionalism.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: We need to have a conversation about the individual whose performance metrics have become... suboptimal. This person of unspecified gender and indeterminate background has been circling the drain, so to speak. We should synergize a paradigm shift in their deliverables, or else they'll be transitioned out of our human resources ecosystem.

jokes.text: French. It's a great language. If you're a chain-smoking acrobat.
jokes.setup: Erin is learning French
jokes.punchline: Her oddly specific stereotype about who French is useful for
jokes.explanation: Good Erin-style absurd logic. The specificity of 'chain-smoking acrobat' elevates it above generic French stereotypes.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm learning French! It's useful if you ever need to, like, break up with someone in a really mean way, but make it sound romantic.

jokes.text: It has a lot of nudity. Which I fast-forward through to get to the chopped-off heads.
jokes.setup: Dwight is describing Game of Thrones
jokes.punchline: He skips the nudity to get to the violence, inverting typical male viewing habits
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight character moment - his priorities are completely backwards from normal people.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I fast-forward through all the nudity to get to the violence. Finally, a show that gets its priorities straight.

jokes.text: Nellie! Get your wrinkly old balls in here.
jokes.setup: Andy needs to call Nellie into his office
jokes.punchline: His completely inappropriate and crude way of summoning her
jokes.explanation: Very much in character for Andy's inappropriate behavior, though relies somewhat on crude shock value.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey Nellie, can you come in here for a second?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I need you to come sit on my face.

jokes.text: I have a thing. A thing of soup. Which I've been wanting to try.
jokes.setup: Jim needs an excuse for having lunch plans
jokes.punchline: His awkward, transparent lie about soup
jokes.explanation: Good awkward Jim moment - his terrible lying when he's hiding something.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I can't do lunch today. I have... soup plans.

jokes.text: Wife of Barack, loves gardening, wants to wipe out fat children.
jokes.setup: Dwight is describing Michelle Obama
jokes.punchline: His bizarrely matter-of-fact description of her anti-obesity campaign as wanting to 'wipe out fat children'
jokes.explanation: Excellent Dwight moment - his clinical, slightly sinister interpretation of benign political initiatives.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Michelle Obama wants to wipe out fat children.

jokes.text: what's your stance on politics? Or, what is the best war to do? And I will just be like, 'duh...'
jokes.setup: Erin imagines meeting Michelle Obama at holidays
jokes.punchline: Her childlike understanding of political topics, asking 'what is the best war to do'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Erin naivety - her childlike phrasing of complex political concepts.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: What is the best war to do?

jokes.text: Yeah, we noticed early on Andy really appreciates enthusiasm. So we decided the best way to get ahead here is to be his cheerleaders.
jokes.setup: The warehouse guys explain their strategy with Andy
jokes.punchline: Their calculated decision to be fake enthusiastic
jokes.explanation: Solid workplace observation about managing up, though not particularly innovative.

dialogue.speaker: Nate
dialogue.line: We figured if we act real excited about Andy being manager, he'll think we like him and go easier on us.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: It's a calculated risk, but the warehouse guys are all in on it.

dialogue.speaker: Nate
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're gonna lay it on thick. Real thick.

jokes.text: It's starting to have this reverse effect, though, where I really do think the stuff he does is awesome. Yeah, me too. It's weird. Hard to remember what's real at this point. Just clap through it, man.
jokes.setup: They started fake enthusiasm as a strategy
jokes.punchline: They've gaslit themselves into genuine enthusiasm, losing track of reality
jokes.explanation: Brilliant psychological observation about fake enthusiasm becoming real. The 'just clap through it' is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: We started fake enthusiasm as a strategy.

dialogue.speaker: Nate
dialogue.line: Now we've gaslit ourselves into genuine enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: We've lost track of reality.

jokes.text: Darryl said, 'cool, man.' He called me a cool man.
jokes.setup: Darryl gives a mild positive response to Andy's news
jokes.punchline: Andy's desperate over-interpretation of basic politeness
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Andy's desperate need for approval and validation.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: That's cool, man.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Did you hear that, everyone? Darryl thinks it's cool! He said it's cool! That's the nicest thing anyone's ever said to me. I'm going to tell my therapist. I'm going to frame that. 'That's cool, man.' Those are the words of a man who cares deeply.

jokes.text: Well, somebody owned somebody. And I don't think anybody would buy an Andy.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Andy's potential slave-owning ancestors
jokes.punchline: Kevin's brutal assessment that Andy would be worthless as a slave
jokes.explanation: Savage burn from Kevin with perfect logic. The casual cruelty is very Kevin.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Andy would be the worst slave ever. He'd be like, 'Hey guys, a cappella!'

jokes.text: Pam, I barely know how to turn on my computer.
jokes.setup: Dwight appears to have done complex genealogy research
jokes.punchline: He reveals he's technologically incompetent and therefore faked the research
jokes.explanation: Good reveal that recontextualizes the previous genealogy claims as Dwight pranks.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've completed extensive genealogical research tracing my family lineage back seven generations.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wow, Dwight, that's impressive. How did you manage that?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I used the internet.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Really? Which genealogy sites?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I googled 'Schrute family tree' and printed out the first image I found. I have no idea how to use a computer.

jokes.text: Right on, brother. Word dat.
jokes.setup: Andy is trying to connect with Darryl about slavery
jokes.punchline: His cringey appropriation of Black slang
jokes.explanation: Appropriately cringey for Andy's character but relies on obvious racial awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yo, Darryl, what's good? I'm trying to connect with you about the African American experience, you feel me? That's real talk, for real for real.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Andy, no.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Nah, nah, I'm serious. I'm down with the struggle, homie. We're like brothers, yo. I respect the culture, fo' shizzle.

jokes.text: Angela insisted that all the animals be fully clothed.
jokes.setup: Pam shows her mural of animals in tuxedos
jokes.punchline: Angela's prudish requirement that cartoon animals wear clothes
jokes.explanation: Perfect Angela character moment - her uptight nature applied to cartoon animals.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I painted a mural of animals in tuxedos.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Why are they wearing clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Because they look fancy.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Animals shouldn't wear clothes. It's unnatural.

jokes.text: Well, if it makes you feel any better, the Dothraki word for 'slave master,' azzafrok, is a term of respect.
jokes.setup: Andy is upset about potentially having slave-owning ancestors
jokes.punchline: Dwight's attempt to comfort him by citing fictional language respect for slave masters
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Dwight logic - trying to help by referencing a fictional culture's respect for slavery.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Don't worry about it, Andy. In Dothraki, the language of Game of Thrones, there's a word of great respect for slave owners.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's... that's not helping.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, it should be.

jokes.text: That you're learning a made-up language from HBO's Game of Thrones? I have a lot going on today, but this was a great nerd-out.
jokes.setup: Dwight expects Jim to be impressed by Dothraki
jokes.punchline: Jim's dismissive but polite shutdown of Dwight's nerdiness
jokes.explanation: Good Jim straight-man response to Dwight's enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've been studying Dothraki. It's a constructed language with over 3,000 words.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's cool, Dwight. Really cool.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I can speak entire sentences now.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I believe you.

jokes.text: People laughed at Klingon at first, and now you can major in it.
jokes.setup: Jim dismisses Dothraki as fake
jokes.punchline: Dwight's false equivalence between Klingon acceptance and real academic majors
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight logic defending his nerd interests with made-up facts.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: False. Klingon is taught at over 200 universities. It's a legitimate academic pursuit.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, Klingon isn't real.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Neither is a degree in philosophy, but people get those all the time.

jokes.text: Hey, boss. I am so thirsty, can I have a scoop of water?
jokes.setup: Kevin asks Andy for water
jokes.punchline: Kevin's weird phrasing asking for a 'scoop' of water
jokes.explanation: Mild Kevin-ism with his odd word choice for requesting water.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Andy, can I get a scoop of water?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A scoop of water?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Yeah, you know, like a scoop.

jokes.text: Diamonds on the Soles of Her Shoes by Paul Simon, featuring, Ladysmith African-American Mombazo.
jokes.setup: Andy mentions his ringtone was changed
jokes.punchline: His racially awkward mispronunciation of 'Ladysmith Black Mambazo' as 'African-American Mombazo'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Andy's racial sensitivity overcorrection leading to awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: My ringtone was changed to Ladysmith Black Mambazo, but I keep saying it as African-American Mombazo.

jokes.text: Pam, I'm related to Tonya Harding. No, I'm just practicing my lying.
jokes.setup: Nellie claims to be related to Tonya Harding
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals she's practicing lying
jokes.explanation: Decent misdirection with quick self-correction.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I'm actually related to Tonya Harding.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Just kidding, I was practicing lying.

jokes.text: My ex behaved like Nixon. All of the lying, none of the sexual charisma.
jokes.setup: Nellie mentions Nixon as an insult
jokes.punchline: Her implication that Nixon had sexual charisma
jokes.explanation: Excellent unexpected twist attributing sexual charisma to Nixon of all people.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: You're being a total Nixon.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: Nelly
dialogue.line: Well, Nixon was very attractive to women, wasn't he?

jokes.text: You're a cocky little thing, aren't you, Pam?
jokes.setup: Pam confidently states Jim loves her too much to cheat
jokes.punchline: Nellie's assessment of Pam's confidence as cockiness
jokes.explanation: Good character observation though not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim loves me too much to cheat.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: That's not confidence, that's cockiness.

jokes.text: Wayne Johnson? The Rock? You mean Dwayne, and no.
jokes.setup: Kevin asks about John Wayne connections
jokes.punchline: Dwight's pedantic correction about The Rock's real name
jokes.explanation: Decent Dwight pedantry but relatively minor joke.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Does The Rock have any connection to John Wayne?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The Rock's real name is Dwayne Johnson, not John Wayne. They are completely different people. The Rock is a modern action star, while John Wayne was a classic Western actor who died in 1979. There is no connection whatsoever.

jokes.text: Dwight's grandfather... Was a member of the Bund, which is not technically the same thing as the Nazi party.
jokes.setup: Dwight reveals his grandfather's political affiliation
jokes.punchline: His technical distinction that doesn't make it less disturbing
jokes.explanation: Dark but perfectly in character for Dwight to make technical distinctions about Nazi affiliations.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My grandfather was not a Nazi. He was a member of the SS.

jokes.text: I was joking about that whole Bund thing. Ho, the look on your faces.
jokes.setup: Everyone reacts with horror to the Nazi connection
jokes.punchline: Dwight reveals he was joking and enjoys their reactions
jokes.explanation: Good tension release but somewhat diminishes the previous joke's impact.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I was joking. I love seeing you all react like that.

jokes.text: We merely transported them. Which, at worst, makes us amoral middlemen.
jokes.setup: Andy learns his family didn't own slaves
jokes.punchline: His relief about only being involved in slave trading, minimizing it as 'amoral middlemen'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe moment - Andy's complete moral blindness in trying to minimize slave trading as merely being 'middlemen.' Perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh thank God, we didn't own slaves.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We were just amoral middlemen in the slave trade. That's so much better.

jokes.text: What if I said that my dad beat me, and I just left out the croquet of it all?
jokes.setup: Andy wants to make his childhood sound harder
jokes.punchline: His plan to lie about abuse while technically being truthful about croquet
jokes.explanation: Excellent Andy character moment - his privileged perspective trying to manufacture hardship from croquet.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was beaten... at croquet. By my father. Every single weekend.

jokes.text: Hiring Scranton's most dangerous young muralist to paint the warehouse wall.
jokes.setup: Nellie offers Pam a mural project
jokes.punchline: Her dramatic description of Pam as 'Scranton's most dangerous young muralist'
jokes.explanation: Good Nellie dramatic overstatement about mundane Pam.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Pam, I have a special project for you. I need you to paint a mural in the conference room.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: A mural? I'm not really a muralist.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Nonsense! You are Scranton's most dangerous young muralist.

jokes.text: She never loved you. / I am gonna find you someone better, and rich. And Filipino. But we'll break that to her later.
jokes.setup: Nellie thinks Jim is confessing an affair
jokes.punchline: Her immediate harsh judgment and oddly specific replacement criteria
jokes.explanation: Good Nellie character moment with her dramatic overreaction and specific preferences.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Wait, you're having an affair? Jim, I'm shocked and deeply disappointed in you.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm going to replace you. And I'm going to find someone who is taller, better looking, and has a more decisive handshake.

jokes.text: I don't know what I was so worried about. I have the best wife in the world.
jokes.setup: The whole episode Jim has been secretive
jokes.punchline: His relief when Pam agrees to the Philadelphia job, not knowing the context
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony as the audience knows Pam doesn't understand what she agreed to.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam, I need to tell you something. I've been keeping a secret all day.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, what is it? You're scaring me.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I got offered a job. In Philadelphia. At Athlead—I mean, the new company.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Philadelphia? Jim, that's... that's amazing! Yes, of course you should take it!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Really? You're not upset?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Why would I be upset? This is what you've been working toward!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I know, I just... I thought you'd want to stay here, in Scranton, and—

jokes.text: I still can't believe he didn't tell me.
jokes.setup: Pam thinks she caught Jim in something
jokes.punchline: Her reaction to discovering Jim's 'secret' driving lesson plan
jokes.explanation: Solid dramatic irony as Pam focuses on the wrong secret.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, what is this? I found this in your desk drawer.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, that's... that's nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It's a driving lesson plan? For me? You've been planning to teach me how to drive better?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I wanted to surprise you. After that fender bender last week—

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That was NOT a fender bender! And you know what? This is actually really sweet. I thought you were hiding something terrible.

tmdb_overview: Andy flaunts his genealogy when he discovers he's related to Michelle Obama. Meanwhile, Darryl struggles in his new post, Dwight teaches Erin a new language to wow her boyfriend's family, and Nellie tries to persuade Pam that Jim's having an affair.
insight: Andy's ancestry obsession fuels 36 jokes in tight 22-minute window at 88.8.
